title: For the first time in history Ukrainian robots captured Russian position and occupiers surrendered
description:
#Kanal13​ #likekanal13​ #subscribekanal13 #warinukraine
https://www.youtube.com/user/kanal13az?sub_confirmation=1 - SUBSCRIBE TO US!

Ukrainian President  Volodymyr Zelenskyy announced a significant breakthrough in the use of unmanned technologies on the front lines. He made this statement as part of his congratulatory address to defense industry personnel. According to him, a unique and unprecedented event recently occurred on the front: for the first time, Ukrainian forces captured an enemy position solely using ground-based robotic systems and drones—without the use of infantry and without loss of life. As a result of this operation, the occupiers surrendered. Zelenskyy noted that systems such as Ratel, Termit, Ardal, Ris, Zmiy, Protector, and Volya have completed over 22,000 combat missions over the past three months. This has yielded a colossal result, saving thousands of lives. In fact, robots were sent to the most dangerous frontline zones instead of soldiers. According to him, Ukraine is focusing on saving soldiers' lives through the development of modern technology. At the end of March, the 225th Separate Assault Regiment of the Armed Forces of Ukraine reported that Ukrainian defenders had used drones to rescue two prisoners of war from the Russian troops. The soldiers released a video showing drones trapping the enemy escort. The Ukrainian POWs were able to escape.
A few days earlier, Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of Ukraine Oleksandr Syrskyi stated that in March, robotic systems completed 50% more tasks than in February. According to him, Ukraine’s Defense Forces maintain the strategic initiative and are preventing Russian troops from resuming a large-scale offensive. One of the key factors behind Ukraine’s advantage is its unmanned systems forces. These units are currently delivering the most massive and effective strikes against the enemy. “For four consecutive months, starting from December 2025, our unmanned systems units have been neutralizing more enemy personnel than Russia is recruiting into its ranks,” Syrskyi said. Thus, in March compared to February, the total personnel losses inflicted on the enemy by Ukrainian unmanned systems units increased by 29%. According to the commander-in-chief, on average, unmanned units now carry out more than 11,000 combat missions per day. At the same time, in March, the number of verified targets hit increased by 50% compared to February — exceeding 150,000.

title: PESKOV WARNS DONETSK FALL: Kremlin Says Ukraine War Now Just 'A Few Kilometres' From End
description:
The Kremlin said on Sunday that fighting in eastern Ukraine is now concentrated within “just a few kilometres,” claiming that Russian forces are close to completing control of most of the Donetsk region. Spokesperson Dmitri Peskov stated that only 17–18% of the territory remains to be “liberated,” adding that the “momentum is clear.” He said the conflict could end “overnight” if Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky ordered a withdrawal from contested areas. Moscow also said talks on Ukraine remain frozen, while suggesting that battlefield developments are shaping diplomatic conditions. In separate remarks, Russia warned of its energy leverage over Europe and said NATO is undergoing structural transformation amid shifting global security dynamics.

title: Ukraine CRUSHED Russia’s Kharkiv Front in 90 HOURS — 9,000 km² LOST | Ben Hodges
description:

Retired U.S. Lt. General Ben Hodges breaks down exactly HOW Ukraine pulled this off — and what it means for the rest of the war.

title: Hundreds of devastating strikes deep into Russia: Ukraine annihilates Russia's economy and logistics
description:
#Kanal13​ #likekanal13​ #subscribekanal13 #warinukraine
https://www.youtube.com/user/kanal13az?sub_confirmation=1 - SUBSCRIBE TO US!

More than 350 strikes deep into Russia have hit warehouses, logistics centers, and energy infrastructure. The Kremlin is suffering enormous losses, despite trying to conceal this in public rhetoric. FREEDOM TV reports on how Ukrainian shelling is affecting the Russian economy and how the regime is trying to avoid mass public discontent .
The Kremlin continues to wage a war of attrition both on the front lines and within Russia itself. Daily attacks by Ukrainian troops on Russian infrastructure pose a huge challenge for the Kremlin, stated Kirill Budanov, head of the Office of the President of Ukraine. Although Russia doesn't mention it publicly, the authorities are facing systemic problems: heavy losses, attacks on the home front, and mounting pressure on society.
"No, they're not complaining. They simply won't do so, because that would be an admission of weakness. But it does have an impact, and they constantly point this out in their reports. It's a problem. Just like their attacks on us—that's also a problem," Kirill Budanov emphasized.
By ignoring the problems, the Kremlin is afraid of appearing weak, as this could increase pressure from Europe and partner countries during peace negotiations. And while Putin is trying to project the image of successful combat operations, Ukrainian unmanned aerial vehicle units have been killing more Russian personnel than they can mobilize for four months in a row. According to Ukrainian Armed Forces Commander-in-Chief Oleksandr Syrsky, Ukrainian unmanned aerial vehicle units carry out over 11,000 combat missions per day alone. Syrsky specifically noted the effectiveness of Ukrainian mid-strike systems, citing nearly 350 such strikes to depths of up to 120 kilometers. These targets include warehouses, logistics centers, oil and energy industry facilities, and Russian army command posts.
"Following the meeting, I outlined the objectives for developing our unmanned systems. We continue to deliver devastating blows to the enemy while simultaneously saving the lives of Ukrainian soldiers," noted Alexander Syrsky. At the same time, Moscow is trying to find a diplomatic way out of the war while saving face. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy states that the Kremlin recognizes that a complete occupation of Ukraine is impossible due to the lack of trained Russian troops and the colossal losses. According to Kyiv's estimates, Russia would have to sacrifice hundreds of thousands of people to capture Donbass alone.
"I believe Putin understands that he can't completely occupy Ukraine... He has suffered enormous losses and doesn't have sufficiently trained forces on the battlefield. He's trying to find a way out of the situation that would look like victory. That's why he's trying to force us out of Donbas diplomatically—through dialogue with the United States," Volodymyr Zelenskyy emphasized in an interview for The Rest Is Politics podcast.
As experts note, for the Kremlin, this war is no longer just a political tool, but a matter of regime survival. In this situation, any compromise or cessation of hostilities could pose a serious threat to Russian dictator Vladimir Putin's hold on power. "Putin has no good options. Even if they say you've been eaten, you always have some way out, but he doesn't, because he... How can I explain it to you? War is Putin, Putin is war. In other words, war has devoured him, and he has become part of it," said Oleg Lisny, president of the Politika think tank. Hoping to salvage the situation, the Kremlin is tightening its control within the country—over the media, social media, and public sentiment. Popular global messaging apps are being blocked, and Russia is offering so-called alternatives in their place. According to the Ukrainian president, there are suggestions that the blocking of social media is a prelude to making unpopular decisions. This could mean either an end to the war or an escalation. Then, Russians will face open mobilization. People will be drafted to the front not only from the country's hinterlands but also from large cities, which will cause serious discontent among the population. "The Kremlin understands how powerful both traditional and social media are. Therefore, they are trying to gain control of a sector that wasn't fully under the regime's control, but was. Why? Because everything that isn't under its control, given that the screws are being tightened, given that the socioeconomic situation is becoming increasingly difficult, given that it's becoming increasingly difficult to conceal this information. Why? Because if it doesn't go through official channels, it goes through alternative channels accessible to citizens," noted political scientist Yaroslav Teleshun.

Helmet cam footage shows international fighters in an intense fight for their survival during heavy close combat with Russian forces as the men where storming a Russian held village during an assault operation on the eastern flank.

The video captures members of Chosen Company, a now-disbanded unit of international volunteers who served in the Ukrainian Armed Forces during combat operations in the town of Pervomaiske, located just on the doorstep of Donetsk City on the eastern front.

At first, the international fighters assaulted relatively unmotivated Russian Storm-Z units and were able to capture about half of the town.

Later, the entire company was sent in to storm the village and seize the remaining half.

However, this time they encountered heavily fortified and well-prepared Russian positions, manned by skilled fighters and supported by a wide arsenal of indirect fire weapons such as mortars and artillery, as well as drones.

Furthermore, the area was heavily mined.

This resulted in the men being maneuvered directly into a pre-defined kill zone with heavily fortified positions, where an intense fight for life and death began.

As mentioned, everything was recorded from multiple GoPro helmet cameras worn by the soldiers.

title: Ukrainian FPV Drone Flew Into a Shelter Full of Russian Soldiers - Then This Happened...
description:
When a Ukrainian FPV drone flew into a shelter full of Russian soldiers, the strike reflected a broader pattern already unfolding across multiple sectors. Recent incidents—from destroyed UAZ transports and supply trucks to high-value systems targeted before entering combat—show how detection is now driving the pace of engagement. In places like Crimea and the Pokrovsk sector, units are being hit not only at the front, but during movement and staging, leaving survivors exposed under continued observation. What emerges is a system where one strike does not end an event but begins a sequence, as damaged assets reveal new targets and expand the chain of pressure. The deeper implication is a shift in how the battlefield functions: movement, regrouping, and concealment are increasingly challenged by persistent surveillance, raising the question of whether any space remains truly secure once detection occurs.

title: Israel-Gaza conflict: What is Hamas? - BBC News
description:
Hamas is a Palestinian Islamist militant group which rules the Gaza Strip. 

Hamas is sworn to Israel's destruction and has fought several wars with Israel since it took power in Gaza in 2007. 

In between those wars, it has fired or allowed other groups to fire thousands of rockets at Israel, and carried out other deadly attacks. 

Israel has also repeatedly attacked Hamas with air strikes, and, together with Egypt, has blockaded the Gaza Strip since 2007 in what it says is for its security.

Hamas as a whole, or in some cases its military wing, is designated a terrorist group by Israel, the United States, the European Union and the UK, as well as other powers. Hamas is backed by Iran which funds it and provides weapons and training. 

The BBC's Analysis Editor Ros Atkins explains.

title: Israel’s war on Gaza decimated transport and even made walking perilous
description:
Israel's war on Gaza has destroyed an estimated 70 percent of the territory's vehicles and roads, leaving transport scarce, expensive and unreliable. 
Fuel shortages have driven the price of diesel from $2.40 to $11.30 per litre, whilst journeys that once took minutes now require hours navigating damaged infrastructure. 
Displaced families, patients and students struggle daily to find seats on overcrowded tuk-tuks and handcarts, and relatives separated across Gaza find it nearly impossible to visit one another.

Al Jazeera Moath Al Kahlout reports from Gaza City.

title: Sudan: The world's biggest humanitarian crisis | DW Documentary
description:
In Sudan, the government is waging a bloody civil war against rebels. An estimated 12 million people have fled their homes, either to destinations within the country or to neighboring states. Many of them are seeking help in Chad.

It is arguably the world's biggest humanitarian crisis - yet the civil war in Sudan has received little attention from the international community. 

The Sudanese civil war continues to escalate: Some 26 million people are starving. People here are dependent on humanitarian aid. But Europeans have cut aid payments, and, following the dismantling of the American aid program USAID, emergency aid workers in Sudan find themselves slowly running out of funds.

Working for the United Nations Refugee Agency (UNHCR), Sani Akilou is responsible for both the water supply and hygiene in the refugee camps in Chad. But he lacks money for even the most basic things. As a result, drinking water is a brown broth, and in some cases there are no toilets for thousands of people. 

Sani tries to get a sooty generator repaired to produce electricity for the pumps supplying water to 40,000 refugees. Meanwhile, the few doctors in the camps are fighting for the lives of the first people who have contracted cholera. If the disease continues to spread through contaminated water, thousands of people are at risk of death.

Sani's colleague Charlotte Lepiniec is responsible for education at the UNHCR. Reading, writing, arithmetic - these things seem almost irrelevant when there’s not enough money to fight hunger, disease, and death. But if half a million children stop learning long-term poverty will become entrenched here. 

Education is one of the keys to overcoming the crisis. But everything is in short supply: blackboards, pens, books, chairs, tables. Sometimes, there are over 100 pupils in a class. Teachers haven’t been paid for four months. Nevertheless, they continue to teach - even though they’re going hungry like everyone else. 

Food rations from the UN World Food Programme have already been drastically reduced. The budget for global emergency aid administered by the German Foreign Ministry has also recently been cut by more than half, to around one billion euros.

The film is a journey into a little-noticed region of the world where the consequences of reduced emergency aid are dramatic. It shows how dedicated aid workers try to save lives, despite having very little money to do so.

title: Sudan's conflict, explained
description:
How Sudan's top two military men turned on each other

Subscribe and turn on notifications 🔔  so you don't miss any videos: http://goo.gl/0bsAjO 

On December 19, 2018, protests broke out in small cities throughout Sudan amid an economic crisis, eventually reaching the country's capital, Khartoum. These protests posed the biggest challenge to Sudan's longest-serving dictator, Omar al-Bashir, who throughout his regime did everything he could to remain in power. Bashir relied on various security sectors to protect him from being overthrown. However, his plan ultimately failed on April 11, 2019, when the country's army, the Sudanese Armed Forces, and a paramilitary force, the Rapid Support Forces, sided with the protesters and carried out a military coup, toppling Bashir.

The end of Bashir's regime brought hope to the protesters, but they remained skeptical about the men who had overthrown him. SAF’s Abdel Fattah Burhan and RSF’s Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo (aka Hemeti) took control of the country after the coup and made repeated promises to hand over power to civilians, which would put Sudan on a democratic path. But they continuously delayed fulfilling their promises and instead turned against each other, vying for power in Sudan. The two men have brutally interrupted Sudan’s pro-democracy revolution.

The fighting between the two men's forces started out in Sudan's capital, Khartoum, and spread all over the country. Recent ceasefire breakdowns have led to more bursts of violence, especially in Darfur, a region still reeling from the aftermath of a genocide at the hands of Bashir's regime.

Watch the latest episode of Atlas to understand how their feud has undermined the democratic aspirations of the protesters and put Sudan at risk of a civil war.

title: Al Jazeera Journalist Or Hamas Terrorist: IDF's Big Claim After Israel's Deadly Gaza Strike
description:
A deadly Israeli strike in Gaza has sparked a major controversy after it killed an Al Jazeera journalist — with the Israeli military claiming the target was linked to militants.

The incident has raised serious questions about press safety in war zones, as well as competing narratives over who was actually targeted in the strike.

Al Jazeera has strongly condemned the killing, calling it a deliberate attack on journalism, while Gaza authorities say journalists continue to face increasing risks amid ongoing military operations.

On the other hand, the Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) maintain that their operations are aimed at militant targets, including members of armed groups operating within civilian areas.

title: BREAKING: Italy Suspends Defense Cooperation with Israel Amid War Escalation | DRM News | AH1C
description:
"Our alignment is Western, but we act accordingly." PM Giorgia Meloni announces Italy has suspended its defense cooperation deal with Israel due to the "current situation" in the Middle East. In a surprise announcement from Verona on April 14, 2026, Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni revealed that her government has blocked the automatic renewal of Italy's defense pact with Israel. Despite affirming Italy’s commitment to its Western allies, Meloni cited the deteriorating regional stability as the primary reason for the freeze. This move marks a significant cooling of relations between Rome and Jerusalem and follows similar critiques from other European leaders, including Spain's Pedro Sánchez. This video features Meloni's full soundbite, the context of the Italian-Israeli security relationship, and what this means for the broader EU stance on the Iran-Israel war.

title: Hundreds of devastating strikes deep into Russia: Ukraine annihilates Russia's economy and logistics
description:
#Kanal13​ #likekanal13​ #subscribekanal13 #warinukraine
https://www.youtube.com/user/kanal13az?sub_confirmation=1 - SUBSCRIBE TO US!

More than 350 strikes deep into Russia have hit warehouses, logistics centers, and energy infrastructure. The Kremlin is suffering enormous losses, despite trying to conceal this in public rhetoric. FREEDOM TV reports on how Ukrainian shelling is affecting the Russian economy and how the regime is trying to avoid mass public discontent .
The Kremlin continues to wage a war of attrition both on the front lines and within Russia itself. Daily attacks by Ukrainian troops on Russian infrastructure pose a huge challenge for the Kremlin, stated Kirill Budanov, head of the Office of the President of Ukraine. Although Russia doesn't mention it publicly, the authorities are facing systemic problems: heavy losses, attacks on the home front, and mounting pressure on society.
"No, they're not complaining. They simply won't do so, because that would be an admission of weakness. But it does have an impact, and they constantly point this out in their reports. It's a problem. Just like their attacks on us—that's also a problem," Kirill Budanov emphasized.
By ignoring the problems, the Kremlin is afraid of appearing weak, as this could increase pressure from Europe and partner countries during peace negotiations. And while Putin is trying to project the image of successful combat operations, Ukrainian unmanned aerial vehicle units have been killing more Russian personnel than they can mobilize for four months in a row. According to Ukrainian Armed Forces Commander-in-Chief Oleksandr Syrsky, Ukrainian unmanned aerial vehicle units carry out over 11,000 combat missions per day alone. Syrsky specifically noted the effectiveness of Ukrainian mid-strike systems, citing nearly 350 such strikes to depths of up to 120 kilometers. These targets include warehouses, logistics centers, oil and energy industry facilities, and Russian army command posts.
"Following the meeting, I outlined the objectives for developing our unmanned systems. We continue to deliver devastating blows to the enemy while simultaneously saving the lives of Ukrainian soldiers," noted Alexander Syrsky. At the same time, Moscow is trying to find a diplomatic way out of the war while saving face. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy states that the Kremlin recognizes that a complete occupation of Ukraine is impossible due to the lack of trained Russian troops and the colossal losses. According to Kyiv's estimates, Russia would have to sacrifice hundreds of thousands of people to capture Donbass alone.
"I believe Putin understands that he can't completely occupy Ukraine... He has suffered enormous losses and doesn't have sufficiently trained forces on the battlefield. He's trying to find a way out of the situation that would look like victory. That's why he's trying to force us out of Donbas diplomatically—through dialogue with the United States," Volodymyr Zelenskyy emphasized in an interview for The Rest Is Politics podcast.
As experts note, for the Kremlin, this war is no longer just a political tool, but a matter of regime survival. In this situation, any compromise or cessation of hostilities could pose a serious threat to Russian dictator Vladimir Putin's hold on power. "Putin has no good options. Even if they say you've been eaten, you always have some way out, but he doesn't, because he... How can I explain it to you? War is Putin, Putin is war. In other words, war has devoured him, and he has become part of it," said Oleg Lisny, president of the Politika think tank. Hoping to salvage the situation, the Kremlin is tightening its control within the country—over the media, social media, and public sentiment. Popular global messaging apps are being blocked, and Russia is offering so-called alternatives in their place. According to the Ukrainian president, there are suggestions that the blocking of social media is a prelude to making unpopular decisions. This could mean either an end to the war or an escalation. Then, Russians will face open mobilization. People will be drafted to the front not only from the country's hinterlands but also from large cities, which will cause serious discontent among the population. "The Kremlin understands how powerful both traditional and social media are. Therefore, they are trying to gain control of a sector that wasn't fully under the regime's control, but was. Why? Because everything that isn't under its control, given that the screws are being tightened, given that the socioeconomic situation is becoming increasingly difficult, given that it's becoming increasingly difficult to conceal this information. Why? Because if it doesn't go through official channels, it goes through alternative channels accessible to citizens," noted political scientist Yaroslav Teleshun.

title: RED SEA RED LINE: Houthis Back Iran, Warns Of Consequences For US And Israel
description:
Yemen's Houthi rebels have issued a severe warning to President Trump's administration and Israel, threatening to re-enter the escalating Middle East conflict if military operations against Iran and the Axis of Resistance resume. Following recent strikes and heightened regional tensions, the Houthi-aligned foreign ministry in Sanaa declared its readiness to launch missiles and drones at Israeli military targets and directly intervene to support Lebanon and Palestine. Underscoring the global stakes, the Houthis have also threatened to target the Bab al-Mandab Strait, a critical maritime chokepoint. This potential blockade could paralyze global shipping lanes, severely disrupt supply chains, and cause international energy prices to surge. As President Trump monitors the volatile situation, the Houthi's stark ultimatum signals that any further Israeli aggression could trigger a catastrophic, region-wide war with devastating economic consequences worldwide.

title: Yemen and the Houthis: Pirates, terrorists, freedom fighters? | DW Documentary
description:
The Houthi militia has been fighting for supremacy in Yemen for years. The conflict has become a civil war, with international involvement. The Middle East conflict has further escalated the situation and exacerbated Yemen’s humanitarian crisis.

Yemen, at the southern tip of the Arabian Peninsula, is one of the poorest countries in the world. After many years of civil war, the country is in dire condition. Cities have been destroyed, supplies are lacking, and there is political repression. A lost generation of children is growing up without schooling, medical care or proper nutrition. Aid supplies are blocked, looted or sold on the black market.

The conflict in Yemen began in 2004 with the uprising of the Shiite Houthis against the Sunni-dominated Yemeni government. In 2014, the Houthis occupied the capital Sanaa - allegedly with the support of Iran. The response was a military intervention, led by Saudi Arabia. 

A proxy war, in which the people suffer: So far, there have been over 400,000 fatalities. More than 20 million people - around half the population - are now dependent on humanitarian aid.

Following Hamas's terrorist attack on Israel in October, 2023 and the subsequent Israeli ground offensive in Gaza, the conflict is spreading. Houthi militias are not only attacking Israel, but also ships in the Red Sea - and thus international trade. The US and its allies have responded with air strikes. The civilian population, already suffering, is bearing the brunt of the continuing violence.
Between blockades, war and geopolitics, is Yemen in danger of being torn apart?

title: Who are the Houthis in 60 seconds
description:
The United States and Britain launched strikes from the air and sea against Houthi military targets in Yemen in response to the movement's attacks on ships in the Red Sea, a dramatic regional widening of the Israel-Hamas war in Gaza.

As witnesses in Yemen confirmed explosions throughout the country to Reuters, President Joe Biden cautioned in a statement late on Thursday he would not hesitate to take further action if needed.

The Iran-aligned Houthis are playing an escalating role in the Middle East, attacking shipping in the Red Sea and firing drones and missiles at Israel in a campaign they say aims to support Palestinians in the Gaza war.

The Houthis' role has added to the conflict's regional risks, threatening sea lanes through which much of the world's oil is shipped, and worrying states on the Red Sea as Houthi rockets and drones fly towards Israel.

title: Mass Rallies in Houthi-Controlled Yemen: Solidarity with Iran, Palestine, Lebanon.
description:
In this video, we cover the massive rallies that took place across Houthi-controlled areas of Yemen, including the capital city of Sana'a and other northern provinces. Hundreds of thousands—possibly over a million—gathered to express solidarity with Iran, Palestine, and Lebanon.
Key highlights from the rallies:

Protesters waved the flags of Yemen, Iran, Palestine, and Lebanon
Demonstrators condemned U.S. and Israeli military actions in the region
The rallies were organized by the Houthi movement.
Crowds chanted in support of the "Axis of Resistance"
Similar marches were reported in multiple Houthi-controlled provinces

Why this matters:
Yemen has been embroiled in a civil war since 2014, with the Houthis controlling the capital and much of the north. These rallies demonstrate the group's strong alignment with Iran and its opposition to Israel and the United States, reflecting broader regional tensions.

Location
Sanna, Yemen

title: Syria - The rise and fall of the Assad regime | DW Documentary
description:
On 8 December 8 2024, Syria's dictator Bashar al-Assad fled to Russia with his family. The film, which was shot before and after the fall of the regime, shows why the dictatorship collapsed so quickly and what hopes Syrians have today.

Bashar al-Assad came to power in 2000, after the death of his father. Together with his western-influenced wife Asma, the couple seemed to embody a new beginning for the country. But reforms failed to materialize and when the population took to the streets during the Arab Spring, Bashar al-Assad quickly abandoned his progressive rhetoric. Peaceful demonstrations were met with bloody repression and poison gas attacks. Over 500,000 people were killed, more than 100,000 disappeared and at least six million Syrians were forced into exile.

Among them was the activist Bassam Ezzedine. In 2013, he documented the regime's poison gas attack on the Ghouta region. As a result, he had to flee with his family. His sister and her family remained in Syria and were cruelly punished. She was arrested and her children were taken away from her. 

At the time of the first interview, Bassam, like the rest of the world, cannot know that the regime will soon fall and he will be able to return home. As the filming progresses, change comes at a fast pace.

The war waged by Bashar al-Assad against his own people has destroyed the country’s economic infrastructure. A few weeks before the fall of the regime, Syrian journalist Faysal risked his life to secretly film images of everyday life in Syria: starving children searching for food in the garbage, and endless queues outside bakeries. The country had been bled dry after 54 years of Assad rule. After months of preparation, the Syrian rebels launched a new offensive on 27 November 2024. Within 11 days, they ended the dictatorship - without encountering any serious resistance.

The new government promised an inclusive administration for all. But as Marie Forestier, Syria expert at the European Institute for Peace, explains: "The real challenge for the new president is to broaden his base. Because so far he has relied exclusively on loyalists from Idlib, who are very conservative." And even after the overwhelming victory, the new government does not control the entire territory: in the south, the Israeli army has extended its occupation to the Golan Heights, while in the north, the Turks are bombing Kurdish-controlled areas on a massive scale.

Syria is still unstable. However, the hopes and expectations of Syrians following more than 50 years of Assad rule are high.

title: Syria: One Year After Assad | Inside Story
description:
After nearly 14 years of civil war, Syria is trying to turn the page on its past.
It has been a year since Ahmed al Sharaa's now-defunct armed group walked into Damascus to little resistance.
Bashar al Assad - whose family held power since 1970 - had already fled to Moscow. 
Crowds cheered the end of a dictatorship - and political prisoners walked out of the most notorious jails in the country, shocked at their own freedom.
But the optimism of that day has now given way to the realities of transition - sectarian tensions, grinding poverty and demands for justice over atrocities carried out by the regime.

So, can the new government bring real change and unity to Syria? And what can be learned from the steps it has taken so far?

Presenter: Dareen Abughaida

Guests: 

Haid Haid - researcher  at Chatham House.

Ammar Kahf - Executive Director, Omran Center for Strategic Studies.

Heiko Wimmen - Project Director for Iraq, Syria and Lebanon at the International Crisis Group.

title: Myanmar's coup leader who set off a brutal civil war becomes president | BBC News
description:
On 1 February, 2021 General Min Aung Hlaing launched his coup against the elected government of Aung San Suu Kyi in Myanmar.
 
Today, in the first sitting in five years, he's been elected by the new parliament to be the new president.
 
But, with the new parliament made up of mostly loyalists and armed forces, questions remain over whether his appointment will be more of the same, with hope for change gone.

title: Myanmar civil war (2021–present)
description:
Myanmar civil war (05/08/2021 - 15/08/2024)
- The Myanmar Civil War is an ongoing civil war following Myanmar's long-running insurgencies, which escalated significantly in response to the 2021 military coup d'état and the subsequent violent crackdown on anti-coup protests.The exiled National Unity Government and major ethnic armed organisations repudiated the 2008 Constitution and called instead for a democratic federal state. Besides engaging this alliance, the ruling government of the State Administration Council, or SAC, also contends with other anti-SAC forces in areas under its control. Hannah Beech of The New York Times observed the insurgents are apportioned into hundreds of armed groups scattered across the country.

title: Myanmar's Forgotten War Explained: The Military Coup, Revolution, NUG, Rohingya | 2023 Latest News
description:
There’s a war going on right now that you've probably never heard of. The war in Myanmar has been referred to as 'The Forgotten War', which began in early 2021 as the military launched a coup and seized power. 

0:00 Myanmar Explained
0:55 The Coup in Myanmar
1:45 History of Myanmar's Military
2:43 The 8888 Uprising
3:30 Who is Aung San Suu Kyi?
3:45 Ethnic Minority Groups & Four Cuts Policy
5:18 Democracy in Myanmar
6:10 Rohingya Genocide
8:46 Why did the Military Launch a Coup?
9:35 Civil War in Myanmar
10:05 National Unity Government
10:43 Crimes Against Humanity
11:48 Future of Myanmar

The military claimed that Myanmar's 2020 elections had been rigged, something independent election monitors and experts have said is simply not true. A state of emergency was announced and General Min Aung Hlaing took control of the country. Politicians and critics of the military were jailed including the nation's leader Aung San Suu Kyi.

The military in Myanmar has a long history of using violence to seize and maintain power. When Myanmar (Burma) gained its independence after more than a century of British rule it didn't take long for the military to step in and take control.

The military controlled all aspects of daily life and violently dealt with anything that it perceived as a threat. One of the most infamous examples of this came in 1988 with the 8888 uprising. The military responded with brutality, shooting live ammunition into protesters in the street.

Aung San Suu Kyi emerged as a leading voice of this pro-democracy movement & was placed under house arrest for 15 years. She continued to push for democratic change with her political party the NLD.

The military also targeted minority ethnic groups to the point of being accused of genocide. Myanmar is made up of a number of different ethnic groups the largest and most powerful being the Bamar who traditionally have dominated the military and positions of power. When Myanmar gained its independence the Panglong agreement promised a number of these minority groups autonomy and a potential pathway to independence. When the military seized power, burmanisation policies were introduced to suppress the culture, language and religion of minorities.

This oppression led to rising ethnic tensions which the military responded to by introducing even more brutal & extreme tactics, like the Four Cuts Policy. One of the military's bloodiest campaigns was in 2017 against the Rohingya people (considered by many in Myanmar as Bengalis from Bangladesh) who haven’t had access to citizenship rights since the 80s.

By the late 2000s the military announced it would be shifting to a more democratic system of government. However, the new constitution ensured the military would keep control over key areas of everyday life and defence and would always hold a quarter of the seats in parliament no matter what. Even in 2015 when Myanmar held its first genuinely open elections since the 60s and the NLD won in a massive landslide against the pro-military USDP party it still wasn't enough to break the military's stranglehold on power.

At the forefront of the resistance is the National Unity Government which is made up of a lot of the people that would have led Myanmar if the election hadn't been overthrown. The NUG formed in early 2021 and began working alongside and getting help from a number of ethnic resistance organisations.

All of this has significantly worsened the humanitarian crisis in Myanmar and the military has been accused of blocking food, medical supplies and other aid. United Nations expert Tom Andrews says the military needs money, weapons, and legitimacy to sustain itself. The military has announced plans to hold elections so it can appear to support democracy with a government it can control. UN member states have been urged to ignore this election and recognise the NUG as Myanmar's rightful government. There have been calls for an international arms embargo as some countries like Russia and China have continued to supply weapons to the military junta. When it comes to money targeted economic sanctions are being encouraged. - Nicholas Maher

title: Myanmar's civil war: Rohingya targeted? | Inside Asian Conflicts
description:
Many Rohingya, one of the largest stateless groups in the world, fled their homeland in Myanmar in 2017 amid brutal repression by the country’s military. Nearly a million ended up in neighboring Bangladesh, where they have few opportunities.

Rohingya have continued to flee Myanmar, targeted not only by the military but also by the Arakan Army, a mainly Buddhist rebel group in Myanmar’s Rakhine State. Now, both sides appear to be “recruiting” Rohingya for their fight, including forcibly taking people into the conflict.
Myanmar says the Rohingya can return to the country, but with a civil war raging against the military rulers, what would they return to? The Rohingya are caught in the middle once again - the latest episode of DW’s Inside Asian Conflicts series.

মিয়ানমার সরকার বলছে, রোহিঙ্গাদের একটি অংশ দেশে ফিরে যেতে পারে। কিন্তু সামরিক শাসকদের বিরুদ্ধে যখন গৃহযুদ্ধ চলছে, তখন তারা ফিরে গিয়ে কী পাবে? রোহিঙ্গারা আবারও মাঝখানে আটকে পড়েছ৷ ডয়চে ভেলের Inside Asian Conflicts সিরিজের সর্বশেষ পর্ব দেখুন৷

title: For the first time in history Ukrainian robots captured Russian position and occupiers surrendered
description:
#Kanal13​ #likekanal13​ #subscribekanal13 #warinukraine
https://www.youtube.com/user/kanal13az?sub_confirmation=1 - SUBSCRIBE TO US!

Ukrainian President  Volodymyr Zelenskyy announced a significant breakthrough in the use of unmanned technologies on the front lines. He made this statement as part of his congratulatory address to defense industry personnel. According to him, a unique and unprecedented event recently occurred on the front: for the first time, Ukrainian forces captured an enemy position solely using ground-based robotic systems and drones—without the use of infantry and without loss of life. As a result of this operation, the occupiers surrendered. Zelenskyy noted that systems such as Ratel, Termit, Ardal, Ris, Zmiy, Protector, and Volya have completed over 22,000 combat missions over the past three months. This has yielded a colossal result, saving thousands of lives. In fact, robots were sent to the most dangerous frontline zones instead of soldiers. According to him, Ukraine is focusing on saving soldiers' lives through the development of modern technology. At the end of March, the 225th Separate Assault Regiment of the Armed Forces of Ukraine reported that Ukrainian defenders had used drones to rescue two prisoners of war from the Russian troops. The soldiers released a video showing drones trapping the enemy escort. The Ukrainian POWs were able to escape.
A few days earlier, Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of Ukraine Oleksandr Syrskyi stated that in March, robotic systems completed 50% more tasks than in February. According to him, Ukraine’s Defense Forces maintain the strategic initiative and are preventing Russian troops from resuming a large-scale offensive. One of the key factors behind Ukraine’s advantage is its unmanned systems forces. These units are currently delivering the most massive and effective strikes against the enemy. “For four consecutive months, starting from December 2025, our unmanned systems units have been neutralizing more enemy personnel than Russia is recruiting into its ranks,” Syrskyi said. Thus, in March compared to February, the total personnel losses inflicted on the enemy by Ukrainian unmanned systems units increased by 29%. According to the commander-in-chief, on average, unmanned units now carry out more than 11,000 combat missions per day. At the same time, in March, the number of verified targets hit increased by 50% compared to February — exceeding 150,000.

title: PESKOV WARNS DONETSK FALL: Kremlin Says Ukraine War Now Just 'A Few Kilometres' From End
description:
The Kremlin said on Sunday that fighting in eastern Ukraine is now concentrated within “just a few kilometres,” claiming that Russian forces are close to completing control of most of the Donetsk region. Spokesperson Dmitri Peskov stated that only 17–18% of the territory remains to be “liberated,” adding that the “momentum is clear.” He said the conflict could end “overnight” if Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky ordered a withdrawal from contested areas. Moscow also said talks on Ukraine remain frozen, while suggesting that battlefield developments are shaping diplomatic conditions. In separate remarks, Russia warned of its energy leverage over Europe and said NATO is undergoing structural transformation amid shifting global security dynamics.

title: The ‘Gaza playbook’: what are Israel’s plans for Lebanon?
description:
After fighting erupted once again between Hezbollah and Israel last month after the killing of Iran's supreme leader, Israel launched a bombing campaign and ground invasion of southern Lebanon, displacing more than 1.2 million people and killing more than 2,000 so far. 

Subscribe to Guardian News on YouTube ► https://www.youtube.com/user/guardianwires?sub_confirmation=1

Israel says it will occupy swathes of southern Lebanon and that homes near its so-called buffer zone will be destroyed 'in accordance with the Rafah and Beit Hanoun model in Gaza', prompting fears of long-term displacement. The Israeli military has said it is targeting Hezbollah infrastructure such as tunnels and military facilities, which it claims the armed group has embedded in civilian homes. Ghia Hajo, who was displaced from her home near Tyre on 2 March alongside her family, doesn't know when she will be able to return home. Will Christou, who reports from Lebanon for the Guardian, asks what the future might look like for Lebanon and people like Ghia

title: Sudan: The world's biggest humanitarian crisis | DW Documentary
description:
In Sudan, the government is waging a bloody civil war against rebels. An estimated 12 million people have fled their homes, either to destinations within the country or to neighboring states. Many of them are seeking help in Chad.

It is arguably the world's biggest humanitarian crisis - yet the civil war in Sudan has received little attention from the international community. 

The Sudanese civil war continues to escalate: Some 26 million people are starving. People here are dependent on humanitarian aid. But Europeans have cut aid payments, and, following the dismantling of the American aid program USAID, emergency aid workers in Sudan find themselves slowly running out of funds.

Working for the United Nations Refugee Agency (UNHCR), Sani Akilou is responsible for both the water supply and hygiene in the refugee camps in Chad. But he lacks money for even the most basic things. As a result, drinking water is a brown broth, and in some cases there are no toilets for thousands of people. 

Sani tries to get a sooty generator repaired to produce electricity for the pumps supplying water to 40,000 refugees. Meanwhile, the few doctors in the camps are fighting for the lives of the first people who have contracted cholera. If the disease continues to spread through contaminated water, thousands of people are at risk of death.

Sani's colleague Charlotte Lepiniec is responsible for education at the UNHCR. Reading, writing, arithmetic - these things seem almost irrelevant when there’s not enough money to fight hunger, disease, and death. But if half a million children stop learning long-term poverty will become entrenched here. 

Education is one of the keys to overcoming the crisis. But everything is in short supply: blackboards, pens, books, chairs, tables. Sometimes, there are over 100 pupils in a class. Teachers haven’t been paid for four months. Nevertheless, they continue to teach - even though they’re going hungry like everyone else. 

Food rations from the UN World Food Programme have already been drastically reduced. The budget for global emergency aid administered by the German Foreign Ministry has also recently been cut by more than half, to around one billion euros.

The film is a journey into a little-noticed region of the world where the consequences of reduced emergency aid are dramatic. It shows how dedicated aid workers try to save lives, despite having very little money to do so.

title: Al Jazeera Journalist Or Hamas Terrorist: IDF's Big Claim After Israel's Deadly Gaza Strike
description:
A deadly Israeli strike in Gaza has sparked a major controversy after it killed an Al Jazeera journalist — with the Israeli military claiming the target was linked to militants.

The incident has raised serious questions about press safety in war zones, as well as competing narratives over who was actually targeted in the strike.

Al Jazeera has strongly condemned the killing, calling it a deliberate attack on journalism, while Gaza authorities say journalists continue to face increasing risks amid ongoing military operations.

On the other hand, the Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) maintain that their operations are aimed at militant targets, including members of armed groups operating within civilian areas.

title: BREAKING: Italy Suspends Defense Cooperation with Israel Amid War Escalation | DRM News | AH1C
description:
"Our alignment is Western, but we act accordingly." PM Giorgia Meloni announces Italy has suspended its defense cooperation deal with Israel due to the "current situation" in the Middle East. In a surprise announcement from Verona on April 14, 2026, Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni revealed that her government has blocked the automatic renewal of Italy's defense pact with Israel. Despite affirming Italy’s commitment to its Western allies, Meloni cited the deteriorating regional stability as the primary reason for the freeze. This move marks a significant cooling of relations between Rome and Jerusalem and follows similar critiques from other European leaders, including Spain's Pedro Sánchez. This video features Meloni's full soundbite, the context of the Italian-Israeli security relationship, and what this means for the broader EU stance on the Iran-Israel war.

description:

This reservoir provides drinking water to over a million people, and its survival averted a catastrophic flooding disaster.

title: Yemen and the Houthis: Pirates, terrorists, freedom fighters? | DW Documentary
description:
The Houthi militia has been fighting for supremacy in Yemen for years. The conflict has become a civil war, with international involvement. The Middle East conflict has further escalated the situation and exacerbated Yemen’s humanitarian crisis.

Yemen, at the southern tip of the Arabian Peninsula, is one of the poorest countries in the world. After many years of civil war, the country is in dire condition. Cities have been destroyed, supplies are lacking, and there is political repression. A lost generation of children is growing up without schooling, medical care or proper nutrition. Aid supplies are blocked, looted or sold on the black market.

The conflict in Yemen began in 2004 with the uprising of the Shiite Houthis against the Sunni-dominated Yemeni government. In 2014, the Houthis occupied the capital Sanaa - allegedly with the support of Iran. The response was a military intervention, led by Saudi Arabia. 

A proxy war, in which the people suffer: So far, there have been over 400,000 fatalities. More than 20 million people - around half the population - are now dependent on humanitarian aid.

Following Hamas's terrorist attack on Israel in October, 2023 and the subsequent Israeli ground offensive in Gaza, the conflict is spreading. Houthi militias are not only attacking Israel, but also ships in the Red Sea - and thus international trade. The US and its allies have responded with air strikes. The civilian population, already suffering, is bearing the brunt of the continuing violence.
Between blockades, war and geopolitics, is Yemen in danger of being torn apart?

title: Mass Rallies in Houthi-Controlled Yemen: Solidarity with Iran, Palestine, Lebanon.
description:
In this video, we cover the massive rallies that took place across Houthi-controlled areas of Yemen, including the capital city of Sana'a and other northern provinces. Hundreds of thousands—possibly over a million—gathered to express solidarity with Iran, Palestine, and Lebanon.
Key highlights from the rallies:

Protesters waved the flags of Yemen, Iran, Palestine, and Lebanon
Demonstrators condemned U.S. and Israeli military actions in the region
The rallies were organized by the Houthi movement.
Crowds chanted in support of the "Axis of Resistance"
Similar marches were reported in multiple Houthi-controlled provinces

Why this matters:
Yemen has been embroiled in a civil war since 2014, with the Houthis controlling the capital and much of the north. These rallies demonstrate the group's strong alignment with Iran and its opposition to Israel and the United States, reflecting broader regional tensions.

Location
Sanna, Yemen

title: Syria - The rise and fall of the Assad regime | DW Documentary
description:
On 8 December 8 2024, Syria's dictator Bashar al-Assad fled to Russia with his family. The film, which was shot before and after the fall of the regime, shows why the dictatorship collapsed so quickly and what hopes Syrians have today.

Bashar al-Assad came to power in 2000, after the death of his father. Together with his western-influenced wife Asma, the couple seemed to embody a new beginning for the country. But reforms failed to materialize and when the population took to the streets during the Arab Spring, Bashar al-Assad quickly abandoned his progressive rhetoric. Peaceful demonstrations were met with bloody repression and poison gas attacks. Over 500,000 people were killed, more than 100,000 disappeared and at least six million Syrians were forced into exile.

Among them was the activist Bassam Ezzedine. In 2013, he documented the regime's poison gas attack on the Ghouta region. As a result, he had to flee with his family. His sister and her family remained in Syria and were cruelly punished. She was arrested and her children were taken away from her. 

At the time of the first interview, Bassam, like the rest of the world, cannot know that the regime will soon fall and he will be able to return home. As the filming progresses, change comes at a fast pace.

The war waged by Bashar al-Assad against his own people has destroyed the country’s economic infrastructure. A few weeks before the fall of the regime, Syrian journalist Faysal risked his life to secretly film images of everyday life in Syria: starving children searching for food in the garbage, and endless queues outside bakeries. The country had been bled dry after 54 years of Assad rule. After months of preparation, the Syrian rebels launched a new offensive on 27 November 2024. Within 11 days, they ended the dictatorship - without encountering any serious resistance.

The new government promised an inclusive administration for all. But as Marie Forestier, Syria expert at the European Institute for Peace, explains: "The real challenge for the new president is to broaden his base. Because so far he has relied exclusively on loyalists from Idlib, who are very conservative." And even after the overwhelming victory, the new government does not control the entire territory: in the south, the Israeli army has extended its occupation to the Golan Heights, while in the north, the Turks are bombing Kurdish-controlled areas on a massive scale.

Syria is still unstable. However, the hopes and expectations of Syrians following more than 50 years of Assad rule are high.

title: How Myanmar's Civil War Unfolded | Battle Board | Daily Mail
description:
On the morning of February 1, 2021, the world watched in amazement as a PE teacher from Myanmar unwittingly captured the military launching a coup against the government in the background of an exercise video. But the footage itself asks more questions than it answers: Why was the military launching a coup in the first place? How did the country get to this point? And what has happened since then? Here, BattleBoard examines the lives of that PE teacher, Myanmar's new military dictator, and The Lady - better known as Aung San Suu Kyi - to explain everything you ever wanted to know (but didn't know how to ask) about the Myanmar Civil War.
